emergent_themes.description: Multi-service subscriptions (like Uber One) create powerful competitive moats by solving multiple problems (transport + food) vs single-purpose subscriptions
Social media drives food discovery and inspiration but doesn't always translate to ordering behavior - there's a gap between seeing content and acting on it
Low awareness of student-specific benefits creates opportunities for competitors to win share when users learn about them
Structured users are most vulnerable to delivery when their routine breaks down - late work, travel, unexpected circumstances

coding_summary: Louise demonstrates sophisticated understanding of the delivery ecosystem with strong ecosystem lock-in through Uber One. Key patterns include: routine-driven behavior with delivery as fallback, high price sensitivity with low switching thresholds, social media discovery that doesn't always convert to orders, and significant awareness gaps around student benefits. Her post-graduation evolution shows decreased frequency but more strategic usage. Strong evidence of competitive arbitrage behavior and ecosystem advantages.

coverage_gaps.reason: Participant didn't express any stigma around solo ordering - she orders alone regularly without apparent concern
